blocks.text: Every journey begins with a first step, and in the spiritual life of a believer, that first step is the intention (niyyah). It is the reorientation of the heart towards its Creator. Without a sincere intention, even the most physically demanding acts of worship remain empty shells. Niyyah is what distinguishes a custom or habit from an act of devotion that brings one closer to Allah.

Niyyah is not merely a thought; it is the firm determination in the heart to perform an act for the sake of Allah alone. It is the filter through which our deeds pass. A simple act like eating can be transformed into worship if the intention is to gain strength for prayer. Conversely, a great act like charity can lose its spiritual value if the intention is to seek praise from people.

blocks.textUthmani: وَمَآ أُمِرُوٓاْ إِلَّا لِيَعۡبُدُواْ ٱللَّهَ مُخۡلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ حُنَفَآءَ وَيُقِيمُواْ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُؤۡتُواْ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَۚ وَذَٰلِكَ دِينُ ٱلۡقَيِّمَةِ
blocks.translationEn: though all they are ordered to do is worship God alone, sincerely devoting their religion to Him as people of true faith, keep up the prayer, and pay the prescribed alms, for that is the true religion

blocks.text: Allah commands that our worship be directed solely to Him, with complete sincerity. This verse emphasizes that the external form of worship—establishing prayer and giving zakat—must be powered by an internal devotion that recognizes Allah as the only one worthy of worship.

blocks.textEn: Narrated 'Umar bin Al-Khattab: I heard Allah's Messenger (ﷺ) saying, "The reward of deeds depends upon the intentions and every person will get the reward according to what he has intended. So whoever emigrated for worldly benefits or for a woman to marry, his emigration was for what he emigrated for

blocks.text: This famous hadith, narrated by Umar ibn al-Khattab, is considered by scholars to be one-third of all Islamic knowledge. The Prophet ﷺ taught us that our rewards are tied directly to what we intended. If a person migrates for the sake of Allah and His Messenger, their reward is with Allah. But if they migrate for worldly gain or marriage, their 'migration' is limited to that worldly goal.

A common mistake is thinking that you must verbally state your intention, such as saying 'I intend to pray four units of Dhuhr.' In reality, the place of intention is the heart. Once you stand for prayer knowing which prayer you are performing, your intention is already established.

To apply this, try to 'check in' with your heart before every major action. Ask yourself: 'Why am I doing this?' Even mundane tasks like sleeping early or being kind to a neighbor can become worship if you consciously intend to follow the Sunnah or seek Allah's pleasure through them.

In the context of Khushu, the intention is the gatekeeper. By focusing your heart before the prayer begins, you set the stage for a connection with Allah that is deep, sincere, and free from worldly distractions.
